The reality is people only buy products after they read or hear words that make them want to buy those products. It's the words that matter. In fact, you can have a terrible looking website, terribly designed Facebook ads, terribly designed everything that just look hideous, but if you've got the right words, I guarantee you you will still beat the products. What are words? Words are ideas that you are putting in somebody else's mind so they begin to have your thoughts. That's what words are. Words are ideas you're putting in somebody else's head so that they begin to have your thoughts. We want to be a very disciplined steward of those words to benefit our products and services, hopefully because our products and services really do help people and improve their lives. Before we get into the messaging strategy, before we begin to clarify your message, I need to explain to you what's going on in the human brain because that will help you understand why this story process actually works so well. There are two things that are always happening in your brain and your customer's brain. The first thing that the brain is designed to do is to keep you alive. It is the job of the human brain to keep you on the planet, to help you survive and thrive. It's the number one task of the brain. What that means is that your brain is constantly running an algorithm. It's constantly saying, what do I need to do? What do I need to engage? What products do I need to buy in order to survive and thrive? In other words, it's asking this, what information do I keep and what information do I throw out? And the overwhelming amount of information that you and your customers encounter every single day is thrown out. It's thrown out. Your subconscious is saying, I don't need that information. Don't pay attention to that. I don't need it. If you didn't have that mechanism, your life would be unmanageable. If you didn't have that mechanism, you'd walk into a Starbucks first thing in the morning to get a cup of coffee and you'd pick up a pound of coffee. You'd say, well, this is a weird kind of packaging. It feels like metal, but it's got the texture of paper and it's got these two little flaps on top. Look, if you pull these flaps up, it kind of looks like Batman. And then, you know, I wonder if this is glue sealed or if this is stitch sealed. Three hours later, if you didn't have this mechanism, you would still be sitting there looking at this pound of coffee. Why? Because your subconscious didn't say, hey, we don't need to understand how this packaging is done in order to survive. What we need to do is grab the cup of coffee with our name misspelled on it and walk out of here, get to the office, and get back to work so we can get a paycheck and pay our mortgage and all the sorts of things that help us survive. Your brain is constantly running that algorithm. Okay, so I want you to write this down. Hopefully you've got a little moleskin or something you can take notes on. The first key, then, to growing our business is to understand this. We must associate our products and services with the survival of our potential customer. We must associate our products and services with the survival of our potential customer or they are designed to ignore us. We must associate our products and services with the survival of potential customers or they are designed to ignore us. Why? Because the task of their brain, the main job of the brain is to keep them alive and keep them on the planet. So if you're not saying my product will help you survive and thrive in some way, you're in a category in which their brain is designed to ignore you. How does this play out? Well, let's say you're at a cocktail party and somebody says, what do you do? You say, well, it's complicated. My grandfather started a company 75 years ago and blah, blah, blah. We're trying to increase our great places to work metric and we've done a lot of research on this particular product. Our spray-on liner makes sure eggs come off of your frying pan and other spray-on liners are, you know, whatever. You're going into your story about your product and your usual routine pitch. What my brain is doing, you guys, what my brain is doing is you're talking is it's saying, how is this going to help me survive? How is this going to help me survive? How is this going to help me survive? It's not. How is this going to help me survive? Their grandfather starting a company is not going to help me survive. That's not information that I need. How is this going to help me survive? The millions they've put into research on this particular product have nothing to do with my life or making my life better. It's not going to help me. And then I think you have eight seconds, eight seconds. And if they cannot connect how you can help them survive or thrive, they will tune out and begin to daydream. Eight seconds. So you've got to start. The first thing out of your mouth has to say, well, here's how we help people survive. Right? Now you say, Don, we don't help people survive. That's not what we do. Listen, if you've sold any amount of products, you actually do help people survive. You're just not sure how. Now if you think about survival, we want to go all the way back to high school or college. Whenever you studied Abraham Maslow's hierarchy of needs, at the bottom of the hierarchy, Abraham Maslow said, you need food and shelter. And then you need social status. So in a first world economy, hopefully, we all, everybody watching this video at least, has food and shelter. Hopefully you're taken care of. And you're OK. After food and shelter, it gets very nuanced, these survival mechanisms. But I guarantee you everything that your customer wants and everything that you do throughout the day has everything to do somehow with survival. In fact, if we were together in the room and I were to say to Manny Torres over here or Eric Wilkinson over here, if I were to say, hey, can I get you a refill on your cup of coffee? I'm going to go get some coffee. Can I get you a refill on your cup of coffee? You would probably say, wow, Don is a really nice guy. I can't believe he's getting me a refill on a cup of coffee. Now if I were to continue to do things like that over a period of time, you and I would become friends. And that's called building a tribe. Thomas, I'll get you that cup of coffee in a second. I'm going back there in a minute. So I guarantee that would happen. But what is really happening? Is Don really being a nice guy? Hopefully I'm a nice guy. I think I'm a nice guy. But what's really happening on a primitive level is this. What's really happening is I'm saying, hey, Thomas, can I get you a refill on your cup of coffee so that you will like me and we will create a bond so that if sometime in the future, barbarians come over the hill behind our cul-de-sac, you will decide to fight with me. That's actually what's taking place on a primitive level. And sure enough, a year into it, barbarians come over the hill behind our cul-de-sac and Thomas goes, well, I got to fight with Don. He got me all those cups of coffee. He's going to put his life on the line. What I'm doing is I'm building a tribe of people that I can embed myself into and my chances of survival go way up. So even just getting somebody a cup of coffee is actually the motivation behind that is survival. The finding of a mate, the getting married, all of that, survival. Status, upgrading your car, your home to show power is a survival mechanism within a tribe. Saving money is a survival mechanism. Making money is a survival mechanism. Becoming more efficient and productive in my work is a survival mechanism. Everything that people buy comes down to survival. Even rest, even a vacation is to rest and recover and come back stronger and stay sane in order to survive. It's all about survival. So we've got to associate our products and services with the survival of our customers or they are designed to ignore us. I'm going to show you how to do that in this framework. It's one of the things we're going to talk about. The second thing that the human brain is always trying to do is conserve calories. We're trying to conserve calories. What does that mean? The average brain burns between 600 and 800 calories a day just processing information. Today, I'm sorry to say, you're going to burn a little bit more. You're going to burn 1,000 to 1,200 calories just processing the information that we're going to go through today. Your brain is the organ in your body that actually consumes, burns the most calories per day. It's not any other muscle group. It is literally your brain. You're going to sit in that chair today and you're going to work out. I promise you. And tonight, you're going to be tired. Your spouse is going to say, hey, how was your day? And you're going to go fine. Did you learn anything? Yes. What did you learn? Lots. Because you are out of energy. You are out of mental energy. Those of you who are riders, my wife, my poor wife, I go down in the bunker in my garage. I spend three hours riding. She comes up, what did you ride on? She wants to talk. And I'm like, baby, I'm spent. Give me a minute. Let me eat some grapefruit or something and get back. So like you go down there by yourself. You don't talk to anybody. You don't have any words left. Yes. They're all on the page. I guarantee you. You're using mental energy. Now here's the obvious problem. If you burn a lot of mental calories, processing information, you're going to deplete yourself of the energy necessary to survive. So what your brain does is your brain says, we don't need this information. You're beginning to burn too many calories processing it. I'm going to shut you down and you're going to go into daydream mode. And daydreams, by the way, when you just zone out and stop paying attention, those are survival mechanisms. It's literally your brain saying, this guy's not helping me survive. I'm checking out conserving energy in case barbarians come over the hill behind our cul-de-sac tonight. And here's what your website is doing. And I'm going to prove this at the end of the day when I actually look at some of your websites. Your websites are literally triggering people to daydream. Your websites are literally triggering people to stop paying attention to you and your products and that is costing you money. So we're going to fix those problems. And here's the key. You've got to communicate how you help people survive and thrive. And you've got to do so in such simple language that nobody has to burn any calories to understand you. Proof? Well, Jeb Bush wrote a book on immigration. Show me jazz hands if you read Jeb Bush's book on immigration. Lanny, did you really read it? Lanny Richardson? You don't have to unmute. Show me jazz hands if you really read his book. You read it. You're the first person in the history of these conversations I've ever had who read it. I think Jeb's wife read it, maybe. I think she lied and said she read it, but she didn't read it. I'm married to somebody like that. So it's too boring, right? We're not reading it. What is Donald Trump's plan on immigration? Let's build a wall. Again, I don't like the plan. It's a doofus plan. It's ridiculous. But what happened? The American public thought one guy had a plan and was unwilling to burn the calories to understand the other guy's actual comprehensive plan. The better idea may have lost. I don't know. The better idea may have lost. Clear messaging will win the day every time, even if you can't back it up. Even if you can't back it up, the clear message is going to win.
